text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Fill the gap:
{{c1::Don't you}} want to come with us?
explanation:
Written: Don't you
Native: doʊn·tʃuː → 'DON-choo'
IPA: /doʊntʃuː/

🔬 Fusion rule: /t/ + /j/ → /tʃ/ (the 'CH' sound in 'chair'). 'Don't' ends in /t/; 'you' starts with the palatal glide /j/ (the 'y' sound). At native speed, these fuse into the affricate /tʃ/. The /j/ sound vanishes — it's been absorbed into the new phoneme.

🧠 Memory hook: 'don't you' → 'don-CHOO' — like a sneeze sound! The new CH is NOT in the written text.
usage:
• don't you → doʊn·tʃuː 'don-choo'
  • can't you → kæn·tʃuː 'can-choo'
  • won't you → woʊn·tʃuː 'won-choo'
  • hit you → hɪ·tʃuː 'hi-choo'
  • meet you → miː·tʃuː 'mee-choo'
spanish: Coalescencia de Yod: /t/ + /j/ → /tʃ/ (el sonido 'ch' de 'chair'). 'Don't you' → 'don-CHOO'. La /j/ de 'you' se fusiona con la /t/ final para crear un sonido completamente nuevo que no existe en la escritura. No es ni 't' ni 'y' — es 'ch'.

text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Fill the gap:
{{c1::Would you}} be able to help me with this?
explanation:
Written: Would you
Native: wʊ·dʒuː → 'WOOD-joo'
IPA: /wʊdʒuː/

🔬 Fusion rule: /d/ + /j/ → /dʒ/ (the 'J' sound in 'judge'). 'Would' ends in /d/; 'you' starts with /j/. At speed, the soft 'd' touches the /j/ and creates a voiced affricate /dʒ/ — the J sound you hear in 'jungle'. The original 'd' and 'y' both disappear into the new phoneme.

🧠 'Would-you' → 'WOOD-joo' — think of the word 'judo'!
usage:
• would you → wʊ·dʒuː 'wood-joo'
  • could you → kʊ·dʒuː 'cood-joo'
  • did you → dɪ·dʒuː 'did-joo'
  • had you → hæ·dʒuː 'had-joo'
  • need you → niː·dʒuː 'nee-joo'
spanish: Coalescencia de Yod: /d/ + /j/ → /dʒ/ (el sonido 'j' de 'judge'). 'Would you' → 'WOOD-joo'. La /d/ final de 'would' y la /j/ de 'you' se fusionan en la africada sonora /dʒ/. Muy frecuente con todos los modales: could you, should you, had you.

text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Fill the gap:
I really {{c1::miss you}} — it's been so long.
explanation:
Written: miss you
Native: mɪ·ʃuː → 'MI-shoo'
IPA: /mɪʃuː/

🔬 Fusion rule: /s/ + /j/ → /ʃ/ ('SH' in 'shoe'). 'Miss' ends in /s/; 'you' starts with /j/. The sibilant /s/ and the palatal glide /j/ fuse into the palatal fricative /ʃ/. This is why 'miss you' doesn't sound like 'miss-yoo' to native ears — it's 'mi-SHOO'.

🧠 Pattern: any word ending in -ss, -ce, -se + 'you' → /ʃ/ fusion.
usage:
• miss you → mɪ·ʃuː 'mi-shoo'
  • this year → ðɪ·ʃɪər 'thi-sheer'
  • bless you → blɛ·ʃuː 'ble-shoo'
  • press your → prɛ·ʃɔːr 'pre-shoor'
  • yes you → jɛ·ʃuː 'ye-shoo'
spanish: Coalescencia de Yod: /s/ + /j/ → /ʃ/ ('sh' de 'shoe'). 'Miss you' → 'MI-shoo'. La /s/ final de 'miss' y la /j/ de 'you' se fusionan en la fricativa palatal /ʃ/. Patrón: cualquier palabra terminada en -ss, -ce, -se + 'you' → fusión /ʃ/.

scenario: Yod Coalescence 🔥 — Tier 2: 'Did you...' question chunk
text:
🎧 YouGlish
Fill the gap:
{{c1::Did you ever}} try sushi before you came here?
explanation:
Written: Did you ever
Native: dʒɛ·vər → 'JEV-er'
IPA: /dʒɛ.vər/

🔬 Full cascade — 3 rules simultaneously:
1. Yod coalescence: 'did you' → /dʒ/ ('j' sound) — 'di-juh'
2. H-drop: 'did' → /dɪ/ (the 'd' stays but 'you' loses its /j/ into the fusion)
3. Schwa reduction: 'you' → /ə/ — absorbed into the complex
4. Result: 'Did-you-ever' (9 letters, 4 syllables) → 'jEV-er' (4 letters, 2 syllables)

🧠 This is why learners miss this word entirely — it's no longer recognizable as 'Did you ever'.
usage:
• Did you ever → dʒɛ·vər 'JEV-er'
  • Did you go → dɪ·dʒə goʊ 'di-djuh go'
  • Did you see → dɪ·dʒə siː 'di-djuh see'
spanish: 'Did you ever' → 'JEV-er' (2 sílabas). Tres reglas simultáneas: coalescencia de Yod ('did you' → /dʒ/), caída de H, reducción a schwa. 4 palabras, 9 letras → 2 sílabas. Este es exactamente el tipo de compresión que hace que el inglés nativo sea ininteligible para los estudiantes.

scenario: Yod Coalescence 🔥 — Tier 3: 'What did you do?' full collapse
text:
🎧 YouGlish
Shadowing drill — say aloud, then reveal:
{{c1::What did you do?}}
explanation:
Written: What did you do?
Native (Gen. Am. fast): wʌ·dʒə duː? → 'WAH-djuh-doo?'

🔬 Step-by-step collapse:
• 'What' /wʌt/ — cluster elision: final /t/ weakens before 'did'
• 'did you' → /dʒə/ (yod coalescence + schwa compression)
• 'do' /duː/ — stays clear (it's the stressed content word)
• Final: [wʌ][dʒə][duː] = 3 syllables from 4 words

🎯 The entire question collapses to 3 syllables. Spanish speakers hear 'WAH-djuh-doo?' and can't parse it as 4 separate words. That's normal — the parse is acoustic, not word-by-word.
usage:
• 'What did you do?' → 'WAH-djuh-doo?'
  • 'What did you say?' → 'WAH-djuh-say?'
  • 'What did you get?' → 'WAH-djuh-get?'
  • 'What did you think?' → 'WAH-djuh-think?'
spanish: 'What did you do?' → 'WAH-djuh-doo?' — 4 palabras colapsan a 3 sílabas. Coalescencia de Yod ('did you' → /dʒə/), elisión de cluster (la /t/ de 'what' desaparece) y compresión a schwa. Los hispanohablantes escuchan una sola palabra desconocida — completamente normal en inglés nativo.
